source_metadata.title_raw: Christine Dietrick - A-19-152 - August 26, 2019 - San Luis Obispo

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Christine Dietrick 
City Attorney 
City of San Luis _Obispo 
990 Palm Street 
San Luis Obispo, CA 93401-3249 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. 19-152 
Dear Ms. Dietrick: 
August 26, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City Councilmember Carlyn 
Christianson and Vice Mayor Andrea "Andy" Pease regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. Lastly, the Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 
18329(b )(8)(A). Therefore, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that 
may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective . 
actions. 
QUESTIONS 
1. May Councilmember Christianson and Vice Mayor Pease participate in future Council 
actions regarding the Property-Based Business Improvement District (PBID)? 
2. When one councilmember is randomly selected to make up a bare quorum pursuant to the 
rule of necessity, does the original selection for purposes of the preliminary discussions remain in 
effect when the matter comes back before the City Council in a future date? 
· 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


CONCLUSIONS 
File No. A-19-152 
Page No. 2 
1. No. Councilmember Christianson and Vice Mayor Pease both have conflicts of interest in 
future Council actions regarding the PBID. 
2. When an official is selected, he or she is selected for the duration of the proceedings in all 
related matters until his or her participation is no longer legally required, or the need for invoking 
the exception no longer exists. 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
Downtown SLO, a 501 (c)(6) nonprofit organization representing downtown business, has 
collected the requisite number of petitions necessary to legally move forward to request that the San 
Luis Obispo City Council authorize a ballot initiative to create a new Property-Based Business 
Improvement District (PBID). The PBID would be funded through an assessment on properties 
located within the district, and fund "downtown ambassadors" who would clean-up trash from 
sidewalks, remove graffiti, conduct outreach to yisitors, and connect homeless individuals with 
services. The Board of Directors of Downtown SLO is expected to move forward with the next 
steps towards formation of this new district. You expect that Downtown SLO will be submitting its 
petition to the city in the near future, and that the City Council will be considering a Resolution of 
Intention to Establish the Downtown San Luis Obispo PBID. 
Councilmember Christianson owns a condominium that is within 500 feet of the recently 
revised PBID boundary. The residence, lying outside of the proposed boundary, will not be subject 
to the proposed assessment or any other costs or requirements of the proposed district. You state 
that you are not aware of any information that could lead to the conclusion that the formation of the 
district could have any material financial effect on her real property. 
Vice Mayor Pease is an owner of a firm that provides architectural and green building 
services. The business occupies a building that is just within the proposed boundaries of the PBID. 
The business occupies the building under a year-to-year lease. You state that you are not aware of 
any evidence that the establishment of the PBID will or is reasonably anticipated to have any effect 
on her lease terms, the business, or the income of the business, but it is theoretically possible that 
any property owner assessment could be the subject of lease renewal discussions. 
You also request advice on the effect of using the rule of necessity. The City has considered 
some preliminary matters concerning the creation of the PBID. The random selection of one 
disqualified member led to the participation of Vice Mayor Pease in prior preliminary discussions 
concerning the establishment of the PBID, and you inquire as to whether a new selection must 
occur for further decisions related to this matter. 
ANALYSIS 
Under Section 87100, the general rule is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public 
official makes, participates in making, or uses his or her position to influence a governmental 
decision which has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the 
official's financial interests, including an interest in real property. 


File No. A-19-152 
Page No. 3 
In order for an interest in real property to be disqualifying, the specific decision in question 
must have a foreseeable and material financial effect on the property. Regulation 18701 ( a) provides 
that a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a named party in, or 
the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's agency. For a financial 
interest that is not explicitly involved in a decision at issue, the financial effect of the decision on an 
official's interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more 
than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701 (b ).) Neither Councilmember Christianson nor 
Vice Mayor Pease's real property interest is explicitly involved in the decisions at issue. 
Councilmember Christianson 
Financial interests from which conflicts of interests may arise are set forth in Section 87103. 
The interest pertinent to the officials in question is set forth in Section 87103(b ): "An interest in real 
property in which the official has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more." (Section 
87103(b).) You note that Councilmember Christianson owns a condominium that is within 500 feet 
of the revised PBID boundary. 
Additionally, Regulation 18702.2(a)(7) provides that the reasonably foreseeable financial 
effect of a governmental decision on a parcel of real property in which an official has a financial 
interest is material whenever the governmental decision involves property located 500 feet or less 
from the property line of the parcel unless there is clear and convincing evidence that the decision 
will not have any measurable impact on the official's parcel. While Councilmember Christianson's 
property would not be subject to the PBID assessment, it is located within 500 feet of the 
boundaries of the proposed PBID. Thus, it is foreseeable that the funding of "downtown 
ambassadors," who would clean-up trash from sidewalks, remove graffiti, conduct outreach to 
visitors, and connect homeless individuals with services, would provide a material benefit both to 
properties within the PBID itself, as well as to those in the immediate surrounding area. Further, 
you have not provided clear and convincing evidence to rebut the presumption that decisions 
pertaining to the creation of the PBID would have a material financial effect on Councilmember 
Christianson' s home. As such, Councilmember Christianson has a disqualifying conflict of interest. 
Vice Mayor Pease 
Regulation 18702.2(c) sets forth the materiality standard for a decision's effect on an 
official's leasehold interest, and provides as follows: 
Leasehold Interests. The reasonably foreseeable financial effects of a 
governmental decision on any real property in which a government official has 
a leasehold interest as the lessee of the property is material only if the 
governmental decision will: 
(1) Change the termination date of the lease; 
(2) Increase or decrease the potential rental value of the property; 
(3) Change the official's actual or legally allowable use of the property; or 
(4) Impact the official's use and enjoyment of the real property. 


File No. A-19-152 
Page No. 4 
Vice Mayor Pease rents a building in which his business operates pursuant to a year-to-year 
lease. Based on the facts presented, the establishment of the PBID would make various 
improvements to the downtown area, where Vice Mayor Pease rents her building, and it appears 
foreseeable that the decision may impact the rental value of the property, as well as Vice Mayor 
Pease's use and enjoyment of the property.2 
Rule of Necessity 
Section 87101 allows an official, who is otherwise disqualified to participate in a 
governmental decision, if the official's participation is "legally required." (Section 87101; 
Regulation 18705.) This exception is narrowly construed and applies only when it is legally 
impossible for the decision.to be made without the participation of a disqualified official, and where 
there is no "alternative source of decision consistent with the purposes and terms of the statute 
authorizing the decision." (Regulation 18705(a), (c), and (d); In re Tobias (1999) 13 FPPC Ops. 5.) 
Typically, this exception is invoked when, due to disqualification, an agency is unable to convene a 
"quorum" of its members. When a decision-making body cannot muster a quorum of members free 
from any conflict of interest as defined by Section 87100, one or more of the disqualified officials -
just the number to make up a bare quorum - are selected to take part in the decision at issue, and in 
any ancillary decisions where the conflict persists. 
When one of the members is randomly selected, that councilmember is selected for the 
duration of the proceedings in all related matters until that councilmember's participation is no 
longer legally required, or the need for relying on the exception no longer exists. The selected 
councilmember will be required to state the existence of the potential conflict of interest as 
specified in Regulation 18705(b ). Additionally, the random selection procedure need not be 
repeated with respect to a series of decisions involving the same general subject matter and the 
same disqualifying interests. (Hopkins Advice Letter, No. A-82-088; Hill Advice Letter, No. A-87-
110.) It would be improper to repeat the random selection where the subject matter of the decision 
is unchanged, as are the disqualifying interests. 
Furthermore, the participating member may only participate in public deliberations 
. regarding the matter and at closed sessions required by law. The member may not attempt to 
influence the outcome of the matter "behind the scenes" by engaging in private discussions with 
other members or staff. (Grunwald Advice Letter, No. A-95-184.) 
Based upon the facts provided, Councilmember Pease was previously selected to take part in 
preliminary matters concerning the creation of the PBID under the rule of necessity, and the request 
that the City Council authorize a ballot initiative to create the new PBID appears to be a related 
matter that will still require the participation of a disqualified councilmember. Accordingly, the 
previously selected official, Councilmember Pease, is the only disqualified official that may 
participate in the related matter. 
2 In light of the conclusion that Councilmember Pease is potentially disqualified as a result of his real property 
interest, we do not further consider disqualification based on any other interest he may have including an interest in his 
firm as business entity, as well as interests in his firm and clients of his firm as sources of income. 


File No. A-19-152 
Page No. 5 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
ZWN:aja 
Sine rely 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
By: 
Zachary W. Norton 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

effect when the matter comes back before the City Council in a future date? 
·
sections.conclusion:
1. No. Councilmember Christianson and Vice Mayor Pease both have conflicts of interest in 
future Council actions regarding the PBID. 
2. When an official is selected, he or she is selected for the duration of the proceedings in all 
related matters until his or her participation is no longer legally required, or the need for invoking 
the exception no longer exists.

actions regarding the Property-Based Business Improvement District (PBID)? 
2. When one councilmember is randomly selected to make up a bare quorum pursuant to the 
rule of necessity, does the original selection for purposes of the preliminary discussions remain in 
effect when the matter comes back before the City Council in a future date? 
·

CONCLUSION: 1. No. Councilmember Christianson and Vice Mayor Pease both have conflicts of interest in 
future Council actions regarding the PBID. 
2. When an official is selected, he or she is selected for the duration of the proceedings in all 
related matters until his or her participation is no longer legally required, or the need for invoking 
the exception no longer exists.
